findings.dominantFinding: The dominant imaging finding is smooth, continuous enhancement following the pial surface of the brain and filling the subarachnoid spaces of the sulci and basal cisterns on post-contrast T1-weighted images. This leptomeningeal (pia-arachnoid) enhancement is often accompanied by hyperintense sulci on fluid-attenuated inversion recovery (FLAIR) sequences.
findings.supportingFeatures: Supporting features include communicating hydrocephalus, which results from inflammatory exudate interfering with normal cerebrospinal fluid absorption at the arachnoid villi. Additionally, there may be areas of restricted diffusion if the infection is complicated by early cerebritis, abscess formation, or secondary ischemia.
findings.criticalNegatives: Importantly, there is no evidence of gyriform enhancement strictly confined to a single vascular territory that follows the '2-2-2 rule' to suggest a subacute infarction. Furthermore, there is no thick, irregular nodular meningeal enhancement pattern, which would raise suspicion for a metastatic deposit.
findings.influentialFeature: The feature that most influences my thinking is the diffuse, smooth leptomeningeal enhancement tracking deep into the sulci and basal cisterns without a focal solid parenchymal mass.

differentials.primaryDiagnosis: The appearances are most in keeping with Meningitis, given the widespread smooth leptomeningeal enhancement along the sulci. This pattern classically results from hematogenous spread of infection to the pia and arachnoid mater and is often the earliest imaging sign of a central nervous system infection.
differentials.principleDifferential: The principle differential would be Leptomeningeal carcinomatosis, however this typically demonstrates a more nodular and irregular enhancement pattern rather than thin, smooth lines. Furthermore, leptomeningeal carcinomatosis most often occurs in patients with a known history of solid organ malignancy, such as breast or lung cancer, which is typically absent in acute infectious meningitis.
differentials.excludeDiagnosis: A diagnosis I would want to exclude is Neurosarcoidosis, given its propensity to present with thick leptomeningeal enhancement that strongly predilects the basal cisterns, and missing this could delay necessary immunosuppressive therapy. However, neurosarcoidosis typically has a much more chronic clinical course and is frequently accompanied by extra-cranial manifestations such as pulmonary disease or enhancing parenchymal granulomas.

management.priority: From a radiological perspective, the priority is to identify severe complications of the infection, such as communicating hydrocephalus, venous sinus thrombosis, or impending cerebral herniation.
management.priorImaging: I would review any available prior imaging to assess disease behaviour — whether this is stable, progressive, or improving. Comparison for interval change is crucial to evaluate the progression of ventricular dilatation or to identify any previously documented primary malignancies that would pivot the diagnosis toward carcinomatosis.
management.furtherImaging: I would recommend a contrast-enhanced MRI of the brain if only a non-contrast CT has been performed, as this modality is significantly more sensitive for exquisitely demonstrating leptomeningeal enhancement, detecting subtle cranial nerve involvement, and assessing for early parenchymal cerebritis.
management.mriSequences: I would specify fluid-attenuated inversion recovery (FLAIR) to detect subtle hyperintense sulci, high-resolution post-contrast T1-weighted sequences to delineate the extent of pia-arachnoid enhancement, and diffusion-weighted imaging (DWI) to screen for complicating acute ischemia or purulent abscess formation.
management.intervention: If tissue or fluid diagnosis is needed and there is no significant mass effect or impending herniation on imaging, I would recommend a lumbar puncture for cerebrospinal fluid analysis to definitively isolate the causative organism.
management.mdtDiscussion: This should go to the Neurology and Infectious Diseases MDT. My report would clearly document the presence and distribution of the leptomeningeal enhancement, the size of the ventricles to assess for communicating hydrocephalus, and any evidence of focal parenchymal complications to guide urgent antimicrobial therapy and potential ventricular drainage.

vivaAnswer.fullScript:
The dominant imaging finding is smooth, continuous enhancement following the pial surface of the brain and filling the subarachnoid spaces of the sulci and basal cisterns on post-contrast T1-weighted images. This leptomeningeal (pia-arachnoid) enhancement is often accompanied by hyperintense sulci on fluid-attenuated inversion recovery (FLAIR) sequences. Supporting features include communicating hydrocephalus, which results from inflammatory exudate interfering with normal cerebrospinal fluid absorption at the arachnoid villi. Additionally, there may be areas of restricted diffusion if the infection is complicated by early cerebritis, abscess formation, or secondary ischemia. Importantly, there is no evidence of gyriform enhancement strictly confined to a single vascular territory that follows the '2-2-2 rule' to suggest a subacute infarction. Furthermore, there is no thick, irregular nodular meningeal enhancement pattern, which would raise suspicion for a metastatic deposit. The feature that most influences my thinking is the diffuse, smooth leptomeningeal enhancement tracking deep into the sulci and basal cisterns without a focal solid parenchymal mass.

The appearances are most in keeping with Meningitis, given the widespread smooth leptomeningeal enhancement along the sulci. This pattern classically results from hematogenous spread of infection to the pia and arachnoid mater and is often the earliest imaging sign of a central nervous system infection. The principle differential would be Leptomeningeal carcinomatosis, however this typically demonstrates a more nodular and irregular enhancement pattern rather than thin, smooth lines. Furthermore, leptomeningeal carcinomatosis most often occurs in patients with a known history of solid organ malignancy, such as breast or lung cancer, which is typically absent in acute infectious meningitis. A diagnosis I would want to exclude is Neurosarcoidosis, given its propensity to present with thick leptomeningeal enhancement that strongly predilects the basal cisterns, and missing this could delay necessary immunosuppressive therapy. However, neurosarcoidosis typically has a much more chronic clinical course and is frequently accompanied by extra-cranial manifestations such as pulmonary disease or enhancing parenchymal granulomas.

From a radiological perspective, the priority is to identify severe complications of the infection, such as communicating hydrocephalus, venous sinus thrombosis, or impending cerebral herniation. I would review any available prior imaging to assess disease behaviour — whether this is stable, progressive, or improving. Comparison for interval change is crucial to evaluate the progression of ventricular dilatation or to identify any previously documented primary malignancies that would pivot the diagnosis toward carcinomatosis. I would recommend a contrast-enhanced MRI of the brain if only a non-contrast CT has been performed, as this modality is significantly more sensitive for exquisitely demonstrating leptomeningeal enhancement, detecting subtle cranial nerve involvement, and assessing for early parenchymal cerebritis. I would specify fluid-attenuated inversion recovery (FLAIR) to detect subtle hyperintense sulci, high-resolution post-contrast T1-weighted sequences to delineate the extent of pia-arachnoid enhancement, and diffusion-weighted imaging (DWI) to screen for complicating acute ischemia or purulent abscess formation. If tissue or fluid diagnosis is needed and there is no significant mass effect or impending herniation on imaging, I would recommend a lumbar puncture for cerebrospinal fluid analysis to definitively isolate the causative organism. This should go to the Neurology and Infectious Diseases MDT. My report would clearly document the presence and distribution of the leptomeningeal enhancement, the size of the ventricles to assess for communicating hydrocephalus, and any evidence of focal parenchymal complications to guide urgent antimicrobial therapy and potential ventricular drainage.
